docs.title: LapLabs Studio
docs.text:
Visual telemetry analysis and vehicle dynamics modeling for iRacing.

LapLabs Studio lets you load iRacing telemetry, build analysis workflows, and visualize vehicle dynamics — all in one tool.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Stint Browser — scan telemetry folders, view session summaries, and select data to analyze
  • 2D Plotting — interactive time-series plots with linked cursors and channel selection
  • 3D Track View — animated vehicle replay on 3D track geometry with ghost cars
  • Dashboards — instrument panels with gauges, bar meters, tire temps, and G-G plots
  • Extract Templates — select and group telemetry channels for focused analysis
  • Aux Math — create custom derived channels with mathematical expressions
  • Signal Filtering — smooth, differentiate, or integrate channels with built-in filters
  • Denoising — automatic one-click noise reduction for cleaner signals

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Installation
  • First Run
  • Quick Start Guide

docs.title: First Run
docs.text:
When you launch LapLabs Studio for the first time, the app sets up your user data folder with:

  • Default track libraries
  • Vehicle model library
  • Dash layouts
  • Extract templates
  • Scope definitions

docs.title: Main Window
docs.text:
The main window is divided into several areas:

  • Upper Center — 3D track plot panel
  • Lower Center — 2D plot panel
  • Left — Stint browser and workflow controls
  • Right — Console output
  • Bottom — Dashboard panel

docs.title: Setting Your Telemetry Folder
docs.text:
1. Go to Libraries > Stint Library in the menu bar
  2. Browse to your iRacing telemetry folder (typically Documents\iRacing\telemetry)
  3. The stint browser will scan and summarize all sessions in the folder

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Head to the Quick Start Guide to load your first session and create a visualization.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Windows 10/11 (64-bit)
  • iRacing (for telemetry data — required to generate .ibt files)

docs.title: Download
docs.text: Download the latest release from your distribution link. You'll receive a zip file containing the application.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
1. Extract the zip to a folder of your choice (e.g., C:\LapLabsStudio)
  2. Run LapLabsApp_Lite.exe
  3. On first launch, the app automatically creates a data folder with default configuration, tracks, vehicles, and dash layouts

Note

No installer required — the app is fully portable. To uninstall, simply delete the extracted folder and the .laplabsstudio folder in your user directory.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks you through loading telemetry data and creating your first visualization.

docs.title: 1. Select a Session
docs.text:
In the Stint Browser on the left:

  1. Set your telemetry folder if you haven't already (Libraries > Stint Library)
  2. The browser scans and shows a summary of each session — car, track, laps, and duration
  3. Click a session to select it

docs.title: 2. Configure the Workflow
docs.text:
Below the stint browser, the workflow panel lets you configure how data is processed:

  • Denoise — toggle automatic IBT timing error correction (on by default)
  • Extract Template — choose which telemetry channels to include (e.g., base, tires, chassis)
  • Aux Math — add custom derived channels
  • Filter — apply signal processing filters

docs.title: 3. Run
docs.text: Click the Run button. The app loads the selected session, applies your workflow settings, and generates plots.

docs.title: 4. View Results
docs.text:
After execution completes:

  • 2D Plot — appears in the lower panel showing telemetry channels over time/distance
  • 3D Track — appears in the upper panel showing the vehicle path on track
  • Dashboard — click a layout in the dash library to see instrument panels

docs.title: 5. Interact with Plots
docs.text:
• Pan — click and drag
  • Zoom — scroll wheel
  • Cursor — hover to see values at any point
  • 3D Playback — use the transport controls to animate the vehicle around the track

Tip

Use Duplicate tab to new panel (below) from the 2D tab right-click menu to compare different views side by side.

docs.title: Aux Math
docs.text: Aux Math lets you create custom derived channels using mathematical expressions. This is useful for computing values that iRacing doesn't provide directly.

docs.title: Expression Syntax
docs.text:
Expressions use backtick-quoted channel names and standard math operators:

@TBdiff = `Throttle` - `Brake`

This creates a new channel called TBdiff with the difference between throttle and brake at every sample.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Expression Description @TBdiff = `Throttle` - `Brake` Throttle minus brake overlap @SpeedKmh = `Speed` * 3.6 Convert m/s to km/h @SlipAngle = atan2(`VelocityY`, `VelocityX`) Calculate slip angle @LateralG = `LatAccel` / 9.81 Convert acceleration to G-force @BrakePct = `Brake` * 100 Brake as percentage

docs.title: Supported Operations
docs.text:
• Arithmetic — +, -, *, /, ** (power)
  • Functions — abs, sqrt, sin, cos, tan, atan2, log, exp, min, max
  • Constants — standard numeric values

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
In the workflow panel:

  1. Select Branch — which data branches to apply the expression to (All, or specific)
  2. Select Channel In — which input channel type (Raw, Pred, Val)
  3. Write your expression in the text field

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Derived channels appear in plots with an @ prefix
  • You can chain multiple Aux Math nodes for complex calculations
  • Expressions are applied element-wise across all samples

docs.title: Dashboards
docs.text: Dashboards provide customizable instrument panels for visualizing telemetry data during playback.

docs.title: Dash Library
docs.text:
The dash library is in the bottom panel. Pre-built layouts include:

Layout Description Base Essential gauges — speed, throttle, brake, gear Tire Temps Four-corner tire temperature displays Vehicle Body slip angle visualization GG Lateral vs. longitudinal acceleration plot Gauges Analog-style instrument gauges MiniMap Track position overview Slip Tire slip visualization

docs.title: Loading a Dashboard
docs.text: Click any layout in the dash library to load it onto the canvas. The dash populates with data from your most recent run.

docs.title: Playback
docs.text: Dashboard elements animate in sync with the 3D track view playback. Use the transport controls to scrub through the session and watch the instruments respond.

docs.title: Refresh Rate
docs.text:
Use the Rate dropdown to set the dashboard update frequency:

  • 15 Hz — lowest CPU usage
  • 30 Hz — balanced
  • 60 Hz — smooth (default)
  • 120 Hz — smoothest, higher CPU usage

docs.title: Pop Out
docs.text:
Right-click any dash and select Pop Out to detach it into a floating window. Options in the pop-out window:

  • Always on Top — keeps the dash visible over other applications
  • Dock back to canvas — returns it to the main panel

This is useful for multi-monitor setups — pop out a tire temp display onto a second screen while keeping the main app on your primary monitor.

docs.title: Removing a Dashboard
docs.text: Right-click a dash on the canvas and select Remove from canvas to clear it. You can load a different layout from the library at any time.

docs.title: Live Dashboards
docs.text: Dashboards can display real-time data from iRacing while you drive. Any dash element that is bound to a variable available in iRSDK will update live.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
When a live session is running, iRSDK streams telemetry at 60 Hz. The live data feeds directly into dash elements — gauges, bar displays, and numeric readouts all animate in real time. The same dash layouts used for playback work live, as long as the variables they reference are available in the iRSDK stream.

Common live variables include speed, throttle, brake, gear, RPM, steering angle, lap time, and tire temperatures.

docs.title: MiniMap Live
docs.text:
The standard MiniMap dash uses GPS coordinates (latitude, longitude, altitude) to draw the car's position on a track outline. These coordinates are not available through iRSDK during live sessions.

The MiniMap Live layout solves this by using LapDistPct — a single value (0–1) representing how far around the track the car is. It draws the car position on a simplified track outline without needing full 3D coordinates. Use this layout instead of the standard MiniMap when running live.

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Pop out a dash to a second monitor for a live heads-up display while driving
  • Use the Rate dropdown to match the refresh rate to your preference — 60 Hz is a good default for live use
  • Not all telemetry channels are available through iRSDK — channels that require post-processing or model inference won't update live

docs.title: Extract Templates
docs.text: Extract templates define which telemetry channels are included in your analysis. Instead of working with hundreds of raw channels, templates let you focus on the variables that matter.

docs.title: Built-in Templates
docs.text: Template Channels Use Case base Speed, Throttle, Brake, Steering, Gear, RPM General driving analysis tire_temps All tire temperature channels (L/M/R per corner) Tire management tire_press Tire pressures (all corners) Pressure analysis wheel_speeds Individual wheel speeds Traction and ABS analysis chassis Ride heights, shock deflections Suspension setup brakes Brake temps, pressures, bias Braking performance imu Accelerations, yaw rate, velocities Vehicle dynamics input Steering, throttle, brake (driver inputs) Driving style input_raw Raw unfiltered driver inputs Input comparison engine RPM, oil/water temps, fuel Powertrain monitoring fuel Fuel level, consumption Fuel strategy weather Track temp, air temp, wind Environmental conditions time Session time, lap time Timing analysis dash Channels used by dashboard elements Dashboard data

docs.title: Selecting a Template
docs.text: In the workflow panel, use the Extract Template dropdown to choose a template. The selected template determines which channels appear in your 2D plots.

docs.title: Adding Extra Variables
docs.text:
You can add individual channels on top of any template:

  1. Open the Extract Set configuration
  2. Use the extra channels list to browse and add specific variables
  3. Use the scope search to filter by name

docs.title: Editing Templates
docs.text:
Templates are JSON files stored in your data folder under extract_templates/. You can:

  • Modify existing templates to add or remove channels
  • Create new templates by copying and editing an existing one
  • Templates are simple lists of iRacing channel names

docs.title: Filters
docs.text: Filters apply signal processing to telemetry channels. Use them to smooth noisy signals, compute derivatives, or transform data.

docs.title: Savitzky-Golay
docs.text:
Polynomial smoothing filter that preserves signal shape better than simple averaging.

  • Window — number of samples in the smoothing window (must be odd)
  • Poly Order — polynomial degree (lower = smoother, higher = more detail)

Best for: general smoothing while preserving peaks and valleys.

docs.title: Butterworth
docs.text:
Classic frequency-domain filter with flat passband response.

  • Tau — time constant controlling the cutoff frequency
  • dt — sample interval

Best for: removing high-frequency noise with a clean cutoff.

docs.title: EMA (Exponential Moving Average)
docs.text:
Simple recursive smoothing where recent samples have more weight.

  • Tau — time constant (higher = smoother)
  • dt — sample interval

Best for: fast, lightweight smoothing.

docs.title: One Euro
docs.text:
Adaptive filter that smooths slow movements but preserves fast ones.

  • Min Cutoff — minimum cutoff frequency
  • Beta — speed coefficient (higher = less smoothing during fast changes)
  • D Cutoff — derivative cutoff frequency

Best for: signals that alternate between steady-state and rapid changes.

docs.title: Integration
docs.text:
Computes the running integral (cumulative sum) of a channel.

  • Initial — starting value for the integral

docs.title: Differentiation
docs.text: Computes the rate of change (derivative) of a channel.

docs.title: Passthrough
docs.text: No filtering — passes the signal through unchanged. Useful as a placeholder.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
In the workflow panel:

  • Method — select the filter type
  • Branch Mode — apply to all branches or a specific one
  • Channel Types — toggle which channel types to filter (Raw, Pred, Val, AuxMath)
  • Parameters — adjust filter-specific settings

Tip

The Denoise toggle in the workflow panel is separate from the filter node. Denoise corrects timing errors in IBT files before any other processing — it is not a signal smoother.

docs.title: 2D Plots
docs.text: 2D plots show telemetry channels as time-series or lap-distance traces. They appear in the lower panel after running the graph.

docs.title: Variable Tree
docs.text:
The left side of each 2D plot has a variable tree with two sections:

  • Segments (top) — toggle individual stints or laps on/off
  • Variables (bottom) — toggle telemetry channels on/off

Each variable can be expanded to show sub-toggles for different data types (Raw, Pred, Val).

docs.title: Interacting with Plots
docs.text: Action Control Pan Click and drag Zoom Scroll wheel Zoom region Right-click and drag Cursor Hover to see values Auto-range Right-click > "View All"

docs.title: Duplicating Plots
docs.text: Right-click a tab and select Duplicate tab to new panel (below) to create a second 2D plot panel. This lets you view different channel groupings side by side — for example, tire temps in one panel and driver inputs in another.

docs.title: Multiple Runs
docs.text: When you run the graph multiple times with different data, each run creates a new tab in the 2D plot panel. Use the tab bar to switch between results.

docs.title: 3D Track View
docs.text: The 3D track view shows an animated vehicle replay on track geometry. It appears in the upper panel after running the graph.

docs.title: Playback
docs.text:
Use the transport controls at the top of the 3D view:

  • Play/Pause — start or stop the animation
  • Speed — adjust playback speed
  • Lap selector — jump to a specific lap
  • Scrub — drag the playback slider to any point

docs.title: Camera Modes
docs.text: The camera dropdown offers seven modes:

docs.title: Follow 1
docs.text: Chase camera that follows behind the car. The camera yaws with the vehicle so you're always looking at the rear. The horizon stays level (no roll). Right-click and drag to orbit around the car. The camera snaps behind the car when selected.

docs.title: Follow 2
docs.text: Same as Follow 1 but the camera does not yaw with the car. As the car turns, you see it from changing angles — useful for watching corner entry and exit from a fixed perspective. The camera does not snap when selected, so you can set your viewing angle and it stays put.

docs.title: Follow 3
docs.text: The camera pitches, rolls, and yaws with the car — you see the world tilt as the car rotates through corners and over bumps. This gives the most immersive "onboard" feel. The camera snaps behind the car when selected.

docs.title: Free
docs.text:
Full manual camera with keyboard and controller support. Move anywhere in the 3D scene independently of the car.

  • W/A/S/D — move forward, left, backward, right
  • Space — move up
  • Alt — move down
  • Shift — sprint (faster movement)
  • Right-click + drag — look around (yaw and pitch)
  • Gamepad left stick — move
  • Gamepad right stick — look around

The camera starts from wherever the previous camera mode was positioned, so there's no jump when switching.

docs.title: TV
docs.text: Fixed trackside camera. The camera parks at a position near the track and follows the car by rotating in place — like a real TV broadcast camera on a tripod. As the car moves away, the camera stays put until it's out of range.

docs.title: TV2
docs.text: Intelligent trackside camera that repositions along the track edges. It picks a spot ahead of the car based on track geometry and smoothly zooms the FOV to keep the car well-framed as it passes. Gives a natural broadcast look with dynamic framing.

docs.title: Drone
docs.text:
Physics-based drone camera controlled entirely with a gamepad (Xbox controller):

  • Left stick — throttle (up/down) and yaw (left/right)
  • Right stick — pitch and roll
  • Left bumper (LB) — tilt camera up
  • Right bumper (RB) — tilt camera down

The drone has realistic inertia and gravity — it drifts and settles naturally. Useful for cinematic fly-around shots.

docs.title: Controls
docs.text: Action Control Move camera in/out Scroll wheel Zoom (FOV) Ctrl + scroll wheel Rotate camera Right-click + drag Pan Middle-click + drag Draw on track Left-click + drag

docs.title: Color Mapping
docs.text:
The track trace can be colored by any telemetry channel value:

  • Select a variable from the color options
  • Adjust the color range (min/max) to highlight specific value ranges
  • Useful for spotting braking zones, throttle application, tire slip, etc.

docs.title: Ghost Cars
docs.text: When multiple laps are loaded, ghost cars overlay different laps on the same track for direct comparison. Toggle individual laps on/off in the segment tree.

docs.title: Vehicle Model
docs.text: The 3D car model is loaded from the vehicle library. The vehicle library includes several pre-configured models with accurate wheel positions and steering geometry.

docs.title: Track Pen
docs.text:
• Left-click and drag on the track surface to draw annotations
  • Useful for marking braking points, turn-in references, or racing lines
  • Use the pen toolbar to change color, width, or clear drawings

docs.title: Stint Browser
docs.text: The stint browser is your starting point for analysis. It scans your iRacing telemetry folder and presents a summary of every session.

docs.title: Setting the Telemetry Folder
docs.text:
Go to Libraries > Stint Library in the menu bar and browse to your telemetry folder. The default iRacing location is:

C:\Users\<you>\Documents\iRacing\telemetry

docs.title: Folder Summaries
docs.text:
Once a folder is set, the app scans all telemetry files and builds a summary for each session showing:

  • Car — the vehicle used
  • Track — circuit name and configuration
  • Stints — number of stints in the session
  • Laps — total lap count
  • Duration — total session time

Info

Summaries are cached after the first scan. Re-scanning only processes new or modified files.

docs.title: Selecting Data
docs.text: Click a session in the stint browser to select it for analysis. The selected session feeds into your workflow — extract template, aux math, filter, and output.

docs.title: Workflow Panel
docs.text: Below the stint browser, the workflow panel controls how the selected data is processed before visualization:

docs.title: Denoise
docs.text: A one-click toggle that cleans up timing errors in IBT files. iRacing's telemetry can contain small timestamp irregularities that cause jitter in plots and derived calculations — Denoise corrects these automatically. Enabled by default — disable it if you need the raw unprocessed data.

docs.title: Extract Template
docs.text: Choose which group of telemetry channels to analyze. See Extract Templates for details.

docs.title: Aux Math
docs.text: Add custom derived channels using mathematical expressions. See Aux Math for details.

docs.title: Filter
docs.text: Apply signal processing to smooth, differentiate, or transform channels. See Filters for details.

docs.title: Track Library
docs.text: The track library stores 3D track models used by the 3D track view. Each track is a folder inside ~/.laplabsstudio/Tracks/ containing geometry data and metadata.

docs.title: Viewing the Library
docs.text: Open the track library from Tools → Libraries → Track Library. The list shows all available tracks. Select a track to see a preview of its geometry.

docs.title: Creating a Track
docs.text: Tracks are built from telemetry data — you drive the track in iRacing, record an IBT file, and LapLabs builds the 3D geometry from it.

docs.title: New Track Dialog
docs.text:
Click New Track in the track library to open the creation dialog. You'll need:

  1. Track name — a label for the library (e.g., "Spa-Francorchamps")
  2. IBT file — a recorded session from the track
  3. Build method — how the geometry is constructed (see below)

docs.title: Fetch from iRacing
docs.text: Click Fetch from iRacing while connected to a live session to auto-fill the track name and sector information directly from the sim. This saves manual entry and ensures sector definitions match iRacing's official splits.

docs.title: Build Methods
docs.text: There are two ways to build track geometry from telemetry:

docs.title: Centerline + Roll
docs.text:
Uses the car's driven path as the track centerline and applies a fixed track width. The roll (bank angle) of the track surface is derived from the car's roll data.

  • Simpler — requires only a single clean lap
  • Good for most tracks
  • Track width is uniform (estimated from the car's path)

docs.title: Left/Right Edge Folders
docs.text:
Uses two sets of IBT recordings — one where the car traces the left edge of the track and one where it traces the right edge. The track surface is built between these two boundaries.

  • More accurate width variation through corners
  • Captures real track edges (curbs, run-off boundaries)
  • Requires two dedicated recording passes

docs.title: Build Process
docs.text: After selecting the method and providing the IBT data, click Build. The track geometry is generated and saved to the library. The 3D track view will use the new track automatically when you load a session from that circuit.

docs.title: Track Files
docs.text:
Each track folder in ~/.laplabsstudio/Tracks/ contains:

  • track.json — metadata (name, sectors, build method)
  • geometry data — the computed 3D mesh points for the track surface

Tracks are portable — you can copy a track folder to another machine's ~/.laplabsstudio/Tracks/ directory to share it.

docs.title: 3D Track View
docs.text: Action Control Move camera in/out Scroll wheel Zoom (FOV) Ctrl + scroll wheel Rotate camera Right-click + drag Pan Middle-click + drag Draw with pen Left-click + drag

docs.title: Free Camera
docs.text: Action Control Move forward/back W / S Strafe left/right A / D Move up Space Move down Alt Sprint Shift Look around Right-click + drag

docs.title: Drone Camera (Gamepad)
docs.text: Action Control Throttle / Yaw Left stick Pitch / Roll Right stick Camera tilt up LB Camera tilt down RB

docs.title: 2D Plot
docs.text: Action Control Pan Left-click + drag Zoom region Right-click + drag Zoom Scroll wheel Cursor values Hover

docs.title: Console
docs.text: Shortcut Action Ctrl+A Select all Ctrl+C Copy
